heading: libro Iudicum: Irruit Spiritus Domini in Samson, et laceravit leonem.
card_summary: This chunk from De Harmonia Mundi presents a systematic pneumatology (doctrine of spirit) organized in eight chapters. Zorzi analyzes the Hebrew word for spirit (ruach, spelled resh-vav-chet) through Kabbalistic letter-analysis, linking its letters to divinity (he), life (yod/chay), and matter (resh = 200). He then distinguishes six types of spirit (natural, celestial, daemonic, human, angelic, divine) and aligns the Neoplatonic triad of Plotinus with the Hebrew anthropological triad of nefesh/ruach/neshamah, arguing that the medial human spirit (ruach) freely adheres to either the higher divine portion or the lower animal soul. Scripture cited includes Judges (Samson), Psalms, Hebrews 4:12, 1 Corinthians, Malachi, Luke, and 1 Kings (Saul). Sources invoked include Plotinus, Hermes Trismegistus (Mercurio), Jerome, and Kabbalistic tzeruf (letter-permutation). The section culminates in an alchemical metaphor for spiritual transmutation and the doctrine of synderesis as the spark of conscience aligning the spirit with its divine portion.
essay:
## Overview and Structure

Chunk 95 covers eight chapters of the *Tono Quinto* of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* (Cantico Terzo), all devoted to a systematic treatise on spirit (*spiritus* / *ruach*). The governing scriptural lemma—"the Spirit of the Lord rushed upon Samson, and he tore the lion"—establishes the theme of divine spiritual power active in human strength. Zorzi's method is characteristically syncretic: he fuses Kabbalistic letter-analysis, Neoplatonic psychology, Pauline anthropology, patristic commentary, Hermetica, and alchemical metaphor into a unified pneumatological system.

## Chapter 1: The Spirit as Mediating Bond — Kabbalistic Letter-Analysis

Zorzi opens with a Kabbalistic reading of the Hebrew word for spirit. The three letters of *ruach* (resh-vav-chet) are assigned three meanings: *vav* (ו) is the letter of divinity; *chet* (ח) is the letter of life (*chay*, חי); and *resh* (ר), numerical value 200, signifies matter or corporeity. The number two corresponds to matter, which Jerome declared inauspicious unless joined to another number. But when material nature is joined to life and divinity, it becomes felicitous. Zorzi connects *resh* = 200 to the Song of Songs (*duecento per i custodi del suo frutto*), where humanity guards the Lord's vineyard with its dual bodily nature.

> Lo Spirito, dunque, è un nesso che collega e connette insieme due estremi, dando loro la vita e la forza, secondo la natura dello spirito che unisce. Perciò risente della natura di entrambi gli estremi che risultano uniti e di se stesso in quanto mezzo di unione, come si può desumere anche dal significato delle lettere che compongono il suo nome.
<cite>p. 2115–2116</cite>

The spirit is thus defined as a *nexus* mediating between the intellectual and the corporeal. It is corporeal but with a most subtle body—not the gross tangible flesh of which Christ says "a spirit has no flesh and bones"—making it easily unifiable with the divine. Those called "spiritual" are those who strive to draw the body toward the nature of spirit inclining to the divine.

## Chapter 2: The Six Species of Spirit

Zorzi enumerates six types of spirit: natural (*naturalis*), celestial (*coelestis*), daemonic (*daemoniacus*), human (*humanus*), angelic (*angelicus*), and divine (*divinus*).

- **Natural spirit**: the medium by which one member acts on another—in man, the imaginative/phantastic faculty in the head moving the hand or foot via multiple intermediate spirits along the nerves. In the wider world, it is the occult virtue by which magnet attracts iron, stones affect human dispositions, and one thing acts on another.
- **Celestial spirit**: the bond linking lower things to the heavens, enabling them to receive celestial influxes.
- **Daemonic and angelic spirits**: both are assigned to every individual human. The evil daemon seeks union with the human spirit via evil works; Zorzi cites Christ's words about Judas as an example of a man transformed into a diabolical spirit through iniquity. The good angelic spirit seeks to make us angels through symbolically aligned good works, citing Malachi's reference to John the Baptist as a model.
- **Divine spirit**: described as a consuming, transmuting fire — *tamquam ignis consumens, conflans, convertens, et deducens quos tangit in naturam propriam, et divinos faciens*. It fills the whole earth (citing Wisdom 1:7: *Spiritus Domini replevit orbem terrarum*), sustains the whole machine of the universe, and ultimately returns all things to the principle from which they came.

## Chapter 3: Nefesh / Ruach / Neshamah — The Plotinian-Hebrew Anthropology

This is the most philosophically dense chapter. Zorzi contrasts the Peripatetic doctrine (man composed only of soul and body, hence spirit = soul) with the Platonic-Hebrew doctrine that distinguishes three parts.

> Plotino, infatti, ne pone tre nell'uomo: il supremo, l'infimo e il medio. Il supremo è quell'elemento divino che i nostri autori chiamano porzione superiore. L'infimo è quello che Paolo chiama 'uomo animale'. Il medio coincide con l'anima o con lo spirito che connette entrambe le altre dimensioni. Tutti gli ebrei chiamano queste tre parti נֶפֶשׁ רוּחַ נְשָׁמָה, nefesh, ruach, neshamah.
<cite>p. 2121</cite>

The *neshamah* (divine portion, *spiraculum* of Genesis 2:7) never sins and cannot be condemned; when it withdraws from a soul destined for punishment, it departs unharmed—like sunlight leaving when a window closes, leaving the room in darkness. The medial spirit (*ruach*), identified with Plotinus's rational soul, freely adheres to either pole: cleaving to the higher, it merits union with the divine; cleaving to the lower animal soul (*nefesh*), it gravitates toward the evil daemon and can become identified with it.

Paul's passage from Hebrews 4:12 on the living Word dividing soul from spirit is invoked to support this tripartite anthropology: the Word teaches us to remove rational affect (belonging to spirit) from animal concupiscence.

## Chapter 4: The Spirit of God and Human Correspondence

The Holy Spirit, being itself a mediating bond between the Father and created reality, shares maximum symbolic correspondence with the divine portion in man. This explains why Stephen, the Apostles, and others are said to be "filled with the Holy Spirit" rather than "with the Son"—the Spirit is more intimately unifiable with human nature.

> Ciò avveniva perché lo Spirito si unisce a noi più facilmente del Verbo tanto che quest'ultimo non si unì all'uomo senza lo Spirito, secondo le parole dell'oracolo rivolte alla Vergine che doveva concepire: Lo Spirito santo scenderà su di te.
<cite>p. 2124</cite>

Zorzi incorporates the Platonic doctrine of the *ochema* or ethereal vehicle of the soul (quinta essentia), allowing the separated soul to suffer purgatorial fire, appear visibly, and experience corporeal joys. He proposes a twofold purgatory: the first purges defilements; the second refines the soul for full divinization.

## Chapter 5: Angelic Spirits and the Hierarchy of Custodians

Drawing on Psalm 91:11 and Jerome's commentary, Zorzi argues that every human being has not one but many angelic custodians, changing as the soul ascends from virtue to virtue. The seven spirits before God's throne in Revelation 1:4 are the supreme intelligences; one of them announced the Incarnation. This forms a complete hierarchy of mediating spirits between the human soul and the divine.

## Chapter 6: The Diabolical Spirit — Hermes Trismegistus and Azazel

Invoking Hermes Trismegistus (*Mercurio teste*), Zorzi states that man, compounded of all powers, has commerce with all of them. Michael governs purified souls ascending to God; Azazel or Asmodeus governs the flesh and blood. Zorzi claims personal empirical witness: he has spoken with individuals who confessed to feeling not a soul within themselves but an evil daemon driving them to blasphemy. He extends this to sexual congress with demons, and quotes the curse of Genesis ("you are dust and to dust you shall return") as evidence that the evil man becomes literal food for demons.

## Chapter 7: Spirit-Possession and the Empty House

The fusion of two spirits — human and diabolical — means that when the compound enters a new host, it does not seek to connect the animal soul to the divine portion but to drag the host's spirit down to its own level. Zorzi invokes 1 Kings 16:14 (the spirit of the Lord departing from Saul, replaced by a tormenting spirit) and Luke 11's account of the unclean spirit returning to the swept house with seven worse companions. The preparation of the heart for the divine Guest (citing Isaiah's *parate viam Domini* taken up by John the Baptist) is juxtaposed with the devil's counterfeit preparation through error and deception.

## Chapter 8: Consonance Between Spirit and Soul — Alchemy, Tzeruf, and Synderesis

The final chapter brings together alchemy, Kabbalistic letter-permutation (*tzeruf*), Chaldean anthropology, and the scholastic concept of synderesis:

- **Alchemical analogy**: the esoteric philosophers describe a medium that first absorbs the nature of gold and then transmutes base metals into gold at great speed—so the human spirit, once united with the divine portion, can transmute the lower soul.
- **Chaldean anthropology**: the Chaldeans called man an animal of "dissolving nature" (*dissolutoriae naturae*) with no fixed image, capable of assuming many adventitious forms—transformable into demon, angel, or God.
- **Tzeruf**: the Hebrew word for spirit, **רוּחַ** (*ruach*), reversed by the Kabbalistic tzeruf method, yields **חוּר** (*chur*), meaning "small hole"—the aperture through which the animal body passes to become spiritual:

> non senza un significato misterioso, la parola רוּחַ, che significa spirito, se letta al contrario, secondo il metodo cabbalistico denominato tzeruf, diventa חוּר, chur, che significa forellino, attraversando il quale il corpo animale assume natura spirituale affinché, unito alla porzione superiore, possa godere della beatitudine.
<cite>p. 2132–2133</cite>

- **Synderesis**: the path to divinization is aligning the medial spirit with the *synderesis* — the spark of conscience that always reproaches sin and calls toward the good. Compliance with synderesis produces interior peace, dilatation of heart, and a foretaste of beatitude, ending with Psalms 73:28 (*"it is good for me to cleave to God"*) and 119:32 (*"I ran the way of your commandments when you enlarged my heart"*).

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This section is central to Zorzi's cosmological harmonia: the universe itself is held together by medial spirits at every level (natural, celestial, angelic, divine), and the human spirit occupies a uniquely pivotal position because it can assimilate upward or downward. The Kabbalistic identification of *ruach* with a bonding nexus recapitulates the macrocosmic function of the World Spirit from the earlier *Toni*. The tzeruf wordplay on *ruach/chur* elegantly condenses the entire soteriological program: the spirit is itself the narrow gate through which matter must pass to become light.
